content: We're designing a new analytics dashboard for our SaaS product. The target users are ops managers at mid-size companies. Main goal: help them see anomalies in daily revenue before customer support notices. What should we focus on in v1?

Ops managers are not data analysts — they want answers, not charts. Design v1 around two things:

1. One 'is anything weird today?' view on page load. A single headline: 'Revenue is -12% vs. same-hour-last-week' in green/red, with a sparkline. If the answer is 'no,' they close the tab and go back to work. If it's 'yes,' the rest of the dashboard is a drilldown.

2. Anomaly detection that beats a human scrolling a spreadsheet. Flag deviations from the seasonally-adjusted 7-day baseline. Highlight which dimension (region? product? channel?) is driving the anomaly. Avoid generic z-score popups — ops managers don't care about variance, they care about 'what broke.'

Cut from v1: custom dashboards, shared reports, scheduled exports. Those are data-analyst features; your users will never build them.
What about notifications? Should we do email, in-app, Slack?

Slack first. Ops managers live in Slack. Email goes to the bottom of an inbox; in-app is useless if they're not in the tool.

Design the Slack message as the whole product experience, not a link. Include:
- The anomaly headline
- The top 2 dimensions driving it
- A 'looks normal / investigate / mute for 24h' button row

If they click 'investigate,' *then* send them to the dashboard. The Slack message should be good enough for 80% of cases to end there.

Do email as a digest only (morning 'yesterday summary'), not for real-time alerts. In-app banners only when someone is already in the tool. Never duplicate — pick one channel per alert.
Our data team is worried about false positives spamming ops managers. How do we keep signal-to-noise high?

Four techniques that actually work:

1. Suppress anomalies below a business threshold. A -30% drop on a $200/day sub-segment is noise; a -5% drop on $500k/day revenue is not. Percentage alone is a bad metric — weigh by absolute impact.

2. Cluster related anomalies. If three dimensions flip at once (US revenue down + Stripe webhooks down + mobile checkout errors up), send *one* alert with all three, not three.

3. Give every alert a 'this was useful / not useful' button and use the signal to retrain thresholds. After two 'not useful' clicks on similar patterns, stop firing that pattern.

4. Mute during known change windows — deploys, campaigns, outages. Pull this from integrations (Linear, PagerDuty, Statuspage) so the user doesn't have to set it manually.

The goal is: every alert the ops manager gets should be worth interrupting them for. One missed anomaly is better than five noisy ones — users will mute the tool if it cries wolf.
Pricing: should we meter on alerts, dashboards, seats, or data volume?

Meter on what grows with the customer's business, not what you give them. That rules out dashboards (a vanity metric) and alerts (you want more alerts = more value delivered).

Seats make sense because ops teams grow with the company, but beware: ops teams are small (3–10 people), so a pure seat model caps you around $500–2,000/month.

The strongest lever is data volume — number of events ingested or dimensions tracked. It correlates with how valuable the tool is to them: a $10M-revenue company has more events than a $1M-revenue one, and they can afford to pay more.

Combine: a base price per 3 seats, plus a usage tier on events ingested. This scales naturally and is easy to explain. Avoid 'per alert' pricing — it creates a perverse incentive for you to send more alerts, which fights your signal-to-noise goal.
